title: The UK Creator Guide to Leaving TikTok for a Platform You Own (2026)
html:
If you are searching for a TikTok alternative UK creators can genuinely build on, the honest answer is that most alternatives simply swap one landlord for another. The real change is moving to a platform where your uploads are minted to your wallet the moment you post, where you take up to 90% of ad revenue, and where subscriptions and views actually pay you in crypto. That is the shift this guide covers, from someone's perspective who has watched thousands of British creators grow, and then get squeezed, on TikTok.

DeHub is built by DeLabs Ltd, a United Kingdom company, and co-founded by Mike Hales and Indi Jay Cammish, who also built First Class Agency, the UK number-one official TikTok LIVE partner agency with more than 1,200 live streamers on its books. In other words, the people behind this platform have spent years inside TikTok's monetisation machine. They know exactly where the money goes, and where it does not.

Why UK creators are rethinking TikTok in 2026

TikTok is still the best discovery engine on the internet. Nobody sensible is arguing otherwise. The problem is not reach. The problem is what happens after the reach: ownership, payout, and control.

Three uncomfortable facts sit at the centre of the decision.

You do not own your audience. Every follower you have is a number in TikTok's database, not a contact you can export, message directly, or take with you. If your account is restricted, shadow-limited, or removed, that audience evaporates overnight. British creators have learned this the hard way through sudden reach collapses that no support ticket ever explains.

You do not own your content. Your videos live on TikTok's servers, under TikTok's terms, discoverable through an algorithm you cannot see. You are a tenant improving a property you will never hold the deed to.

The payout maths is thin. TikTok's Creator Rewards Programme is the higher-paying successor to the old Creativity Programme, and the UK is actually one of the better-paying markets in Europe. Even so, widely reported 2026 figures put UK payouts at roughly £1.00 to £1.30 per 1,000 qualified views, and that is before you account for the eligibility hurdles: around 10,000 followers, 100,000 qualified views in the previous 30 days, and videos that must run at least a minute to count. Rates are not published by TikTok and vary by niche and audience, so treat any single number as a moving target. But the shape of it is clear enough: you need enormous view counts to earn modest money, and the platform sets the rate unilaterally.

None of this makes TikTok useless. It makes TikTok a top of funnel, not a home. The smart 2026 play for British crypto creators is to keep using TikTok for discovery while building an owned base somewhere the economics favour you.

What "a platform you own" actually means

"Ownership" gets thrown around loosely, so let us be concrete. On DeHub, the decentralised social network, ownership is not a marketing word. It is enforced by how the product is built.

When you upload, the content is minted on-chain to your wallet at the moment of posting. That record is yours. The feed is chronological and transparent, so there is no opaque algorithm silently deciding whether your work gets seen. And because the platform is open-source and censorship-resistant, no single company can quietly rewrite the rules under you.

If the idea of moving from a corporate feed to a user-owned one is new to you, our explainer on decentralised social media for UK audiences walks through the mechanics without the jargon.

The monetisation gap: how a 90% revenue-share creator platform changes the maths

This is where leaving TikTok for Web3 stops being philosophical and starts being financial.

Up to 90% ad-revenue share

TikTok decides your cut and does not disclose the formula. DeHub commits up to 90% of ad revenue back to the ecosystem and the creators in it. You do not need a five-figure follower count to switch the tap on, and the percentage is not a mystery. For anyone who has stared at a Creator Rewards dashboard wondering why 200,000 views bought them a takeaway, a transparent, majority share is a different sport.

A TikTok alternative that pays crypto for attention

DeHub runs watch-to-earn bounties: creators can attach rewards that pay viewers in crypto for genuinely watching, which pulls real attention to your work instead of hollow, algorithm-farmed impressions. If you are new to the model, start with our pillar on what watch-to-earn actually is. It reframes the relationship: attention becomes something you can reward directly, rather than something a platform harvests and resells.

Payouts settle in $DHB, the ecosystem token, which lives primarily on Base, an Ethereum Layer 2, with contracts also on BNB Chain and Polygon. It is tradable on decentralised exchanges and on Coinbase, and listed on CoinGecko and CoinMarketCap. That means the value you earn is portable and liquid, not points stuck inside one app.

Tradable subscriptions with resale royalties

This is the feature TikTok has no answer to. On DeHub, subscriptions are minted on-chain and are tradable, and when a subscription is resold the original creator earns a royalty. So a fan who backed you early can pass their subscription on, and you keep earning from that secondary sale. It turns a monthly membership into a genuine asset with a life beyond the first transaction. Our glossary on tokenised subscriptions explained breaks down exactly how the resale royalty flows back to you.

Tokenised uploads: free, pay-per-view, or gated

Every upload can be set free, pay-per-view, or token-gated, so you decide the business model per piece of content rather than accepting one blunt platform-wide rule. A tutorial can be free to grow reach; a premium breakdown can sit behind a pay-per-view wall; a community drop can be gated to subscribers. You hold the dial.

Live streaming without the Twitch-or-TikTok trap

If live is your format, this matters. DeHub's live streaming runs on Livepeer and has scaled to more than 50,000 concurrent viewers, with on-chain live streams and animated tips that land directly, without a platform clipping a heavy cut on the way through.

Given that the founders built the UK's leading TikTok LIVE agency, live is not a bolt-on here, it is a core competency. If you currently earn through TikTok LIVE gifts, read our breakdown of Web3 live streaming as a decentralised Twitch alternative to see how tips and ownership work when the middleman is thinner.

A practical migration plan for British creators

You do not need to burn your TikTok down. Leaving well is a gradual, low-risk process. Here is the sequence that works.

1. Keep TikTok as your discovery layer

Do not delete anything. TikTok remains an unmatched top-of-funnel for finding new people. The goal is to stop treating it as the destination and start treating it as the shop window.

2. Set up your owned home base

Create your account on the DeHub app or via Google Play. Set up your wallet, claim your profile, and post a few pieces so the space is not empty when your audience arrives. Because uploads mint to you on the spot, you are building equity from your very first post.

3. Repurpose, do not just cross-post

Take your best-performing TikToks and re-cut them for DeHub, then add the layer TikTok cannot offer: a pay-per-view premium version, a token-gated extended cut, or a watch-to-earn bounty on the free version. Give people a concrete reason to follow you across, not just the same clip twice.

4. Drive the migration deliberately

Put your DeHub handle in your TikTok bio, say it out loud in videos, and pin a call to action. The creators who move audiences successfully are the ones who ask directly and repeatedly, not the ones who hope followers find them.

5. Turn on the earning features in order

Start with tokenised uploads to establish your pricing, add tradable subscriptions to build recurring income, then layer watch-to-earn bounties to accelerate reach. Introduce them steadily so your audience learns each mechanic rather than facing all of it at once.

6. Keep your UK tax house in order

Crypto earnings are taxable in the UK, and HMRC treats income and gains differently depending on how you receive and dispose of tokens. Rules evolve, so keep clean records of what you earn and when, and take proper advice for your situation rather than guessing. Treat it exactly as seriously as you would any other self-employed creator income.

Is this actually for you? An honest read

A TikTok alternative UK creators own is not a magic money button, and it would be dishonest to sell it as one. If your entire strategy depends on a single viral moment and you never intend to build a returning audience, any platform will struggle to pay you well.

But if you are the kind of British creator who wants to own the relationship, set your own prices, earn a majority revenue share, and hold assets you can actually move, the case for building on DeHub in 2026 is strong, and it gets stronger the earlier you plant your flag. The creators who own their base now are the ones who will not be at the mercy of the next algorithm change.

Ready to build somewhere you own? Start on DeHub and claim your handle before someone else does.

Frequently asked questions

Is there a TikTok alternative in the UK that actually pays creators in crypto?

Yes. DeHub is a UK-built platform, from DeLabs Ltd, that pays creators in $DHB through watch-to-earn bounties, tokenised uploads, tradable subscriptions, and up to a 90% ad-revenue share. Earnings settle in a liquid ecosystem token rather than closed platform points, so the value is portable off the app.

How does DeHub's revenue share compare with TikTok Creator Rewards?

TikTok sets your rate privately, and widely reported 2026 UK payouts sit around £1.00 to £1.30 per 1,000 qualified views once you clear the eligibility thresholds. DeHub commits up to 90% of ad revenue back to the ecosystem, and the percentage is transparent rather than a hidden formula. Always treat any single view-rate figure as an estimate that shifts by niche and audience.

Do I have to leave TikTok completely to move to DeHub?

No, and you probably should not rush to. The sensible approach is to keep TikTok as your discovery engine while building your owned home base on DeHub, then drive followers across deliberately with bio links, on-screen mentions, and repurposed premium content.

Who is behind DeHub, and why should a UK creator trust it?

DeHub is built by DeLabs Ltd, a United Kingdom company, and co-founded by Mike Hales and Indi Jay Cammish, who also founded First Class Agency, the UK number-one official TikTok LIVE partner agency with over 1,200 live streamers. The team has deep, first-hand knowledge of how TikTok monetisation works, which shaped how DeHub's creator economics were designed.

Is DeHub the same as DePaul University's DeHUB or Rowan University's DEHub?

No. DeHub at dehub.io is a Web3 decentralised social network for creators. It is unrelated to DePaul University's "DeHUB" student portal and to Rowan University's "DEHub" digital engineering hub, which are separate academic services that happen to share a similar name.

Are crypto earnings from DeHub taxable in the UK?

Generally, yes. HMRC treats crypto income and capital gains under existing tax rules, and the specifics depend on how you earn and dispose of tokens. Keep accurate records of your earnings and take professional advice for your circumstances, exactly as you would with any other self-employed creator income.
